critic_raw: Several concerns with this design. The implicit feedback label construction relies heavily on click-through data, which is known to be noisy and popularity-biased. While positions 1-2 are excluded from training, clicks at positions 3-10 are still subject to presentation bias — items at position 3 are more likely to be clicked than position 10 regardless of relevance. The purchase-signal weighting (doubling grade for purchases) conflates purchasing behavior with relevance — expensive items or items on promotion may be purchased but not inherently more relevant to the search query. The seller quality features are computed weekly, but seller cancellation rate over 18 months may reflect genuine service quality changes that need careful handling. The evaluation on NDCG@10 with implicit labels may not reflect true relevance from the user's perspective — satisfaction surveys or explicit judgments would be more reliable.
defender_raw: The concerns raised are well-addressed by the design. Residual position bias in positions 3-10 is a known challenge in implicit feedback LTR; the design addresses this with the IPW-adjusted NDCG secondary metric, which weights clicks by inverse propensity of being in each position. The purchase-to-relevance mapping is an acknowledged simplification — the doubled weight is a tuned hyperparameter, and the seller quality lift metric tests whether seller signals (including promotional activity) drive results rather than semantic relevance. The weekly seller quality signals are treated as features reflecting the state of the world at prediction time, which is exactly how they would operate in production. The scope statement explicitly acknowledges that user satisfaction requires A/B testing — NDCG on implicit labels is the appropriate offline metric for the ranking task at hand. All concerns are either addressed by existing controls or represent known, acknowledged limitations.
adjudication_raw: Critic raised residual position bias, purchase-relevance conflation, seller signal drift, and implicit label reliability. Defender's rebuttals were strong: IPW addresses residual position bias, purchase weight is tuned and seller quality metrics cross-check, weekly features match production, and scope statement acknowledges the implicit label limitation. No unaddressed methodological flaw. Verdict: defense_wins.
